core_behavior: The system must be able to handle increased load and demand without a significant decrease in performance.
The system must be able to add or remove resources (e.g., servers, processors, memory) as needed to maintain a consistent level of service.
inputs: The system will accept a variable number of concurrent user requests.
The system will accept a variable amount of data to be processed.
The system will accept configuration inputs to scale up or down (e.g., adding or removing servers).
outputs: The system will return responses to user requests in a timely manner, regardless of the current load.
The system will maintain a consistent level of performance, measured by metrics such as response time, throughput, and error rate.
The system will provide monitoring and logging data to support scaling decisions.
assumptions: We assume that the system will be designed using a distributed architecture, with multiple servers and load balancers to distribute the load.
We assume that the system will use cloud-based infrastructure, allowing for easy addition or removal of resources as needed.
We assume that the system will have automated monitoring and scaling tools to detect changes in load and adjust resources accordingly.
We assume that the system will have a defined set of key performance indicators (KPIs) to measure performance and guide scaling decisions.
failure_modes: If the system is unable to scale to meet increased demand, it may become unresponsive or return errors to users.
If the system is not properly configured for scaling, it may not be able to add or remove resources as needed, leading to decreased performance.
If the system experiences a failure in one or more components, it may not be able to scale effectively, leading to decreased performance or downtime.
edge_cases: The system must be able to handle sudden, unexpected spikes in demand (e.g., a viral social media post).
The system must be able to handle long-term, sustained increases in demand (e.g., a growing user base).
The system must be able to handle changes in demand that are not uniform across all components (e.g., increased demand for a specific feature).
The system must be able to handle scenarios where scaling up is not possible (e.g., due to resource constraints or budget limitations).
security_constraints: The system must maintain the security and integrity of user data, even when scaling up or down.
The system must ensure that scaling does not introduce new security vulnerabilities (e.g., by exposing sensitive data or introducing untrusted components).
The system must comply with relevant security regulations and standards (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).
performance_constraints: The system must maintain a response time of less than 500ms for 95% of user requests, even under heavy load.
The system must be able to handle at least 10,000 concurrent user requests without a significant decrease in performance.
The system must be able to process at least 100 transactions per second, with a latency of less than 100ms.
The system must be able to recover from failures and maintain performance within 5 minutes of a failure event.
